title: Diagrams & Clinical Algorithms
text: Quarto renders Mermaid and Graphviz (DOT) from plain text. Edit the logic, re-render, and the diagram updates — ideal for versioned clinical algorithms.

title: Diagrams & Clinical Algorithms
text:
Mermaid: microcytic anaemia work-up






flowchart TD
    A[Microcytic anaemia<br/>MCV < 80 fL] --> B{Ferritin}
    B -->|Low| C[Iron deficiency]
    B -->|Normal / high| D{Investigate further}
    C --> C1[Find the source:<br/>GI loss, diet, malabsorption]
    D --> E[Serum iron / TIBC]
    D --> F[Haemoglobinopathy screen]
    F -->|HbA2 raised| G[β-thalassaemia trait]
    F -->|Normal| H[Anaemia of chronic disease<br/>or sideroblastic]
    style A fill:#B71C1C,color:#fff
    style C fill:#ffd5d5
    style G fill:#ffd5d5




Figure 1: Simplified diagnostic pathway for microcytic anaemia.

Tip



Because these are text, they diff cleanly in git and never go stale against an exported PNG.

title: Python Engine — Jupyter
text: The Jupyter engine runs Python code blocks at render time and embeds the results. This page uses numpy, scipy and matplotlib from the dedicated ~/.venvs/quarto environment.

title: Python Engine — Jupyter
text:
A worked example: one-compartment pharmacokinetics
We model plasma concentration after an IV bolus, \(C(t) = \dfrac{D}{V_d}\,e^{-k_e t}\), with \(k_e = \ln 2 / t_{1/2}\).

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

dose, Vd, t_half = 1000.0, 50.0, 12.0     # mg, L, hours
ke = np.log(2) / t_half
t  = np.linspace(0, 48, 400)
C  = (dose / Vd) * np.exp(-ke * t)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.2))
ax.plot(t, C, color="#B71C1C", lw=2.2)
ax.axhline(5, ls="--", color="grey", label="MEC (5 mg/L)")
ax.fill_between(t, C, 5, where=(C > 5), alpha=0.12, color="#B71C1C")
ax.set(xlabel="Time (h)", ylabel="Concentration (mg/L)",
       title="One-compartment IV bolus decay")
ax.legend(); ax.grid(alpha=.3)
plt.show()







Figure 1: Plasma concentration–time curve after a 1000 mg IV bolus.





Figure Figure 1 is cross-referenced automatically.

title: Python Engine — Jupyter
text:
Computation: AUC by numerical integration

from scipy.integrate import trapezoid
auc = trapezoid(C, t)
above_mec = t[C > 5][-1]
print(f"AUC(0–48h)      = {auc:8.1f} mg·h/L")
print(f"Time above MEC  = {above_mec:8.1f} h")

AUC(0–48h)      =    324.6 mg·h/L
Time above MEC  =     23.9 h

title: Python Engine — Jupyter
text:
A computed table

import pandas as pd
sample_t = [0, 6, 12, 24, 36, 48]
pd.DataFrame({
    "Time (h)": sample_t,
    "Concentration (mg/L)": [round((dose/Vd)*np.exp(-ke*x), 2) for x in sample_t],
    "Fraction remaining": [round(np.exp(-ke*x), 3) for x in sample_t],
})

title: Julia Engine
text: The Julia engine runs Julia via IJulia. Great when an algorithm needs raw numerical speed. Plots use Plots.jl (GR backend). We set GKSwstype=100 so GR renders straight to a file on a headless machine.

title: Julia Engine
section: A worked example: IV bolus decay
text:
A worked example: IV bolus decay

using Plots
ENV["GKSwstype"] = "100"   # headless GR: write to file, no display server
gr()

dose, Vd, thalf = 1000.0, 50.0, 12.0
ke = log(2) / thalf
t  = 0:0.5:48
C  = (dose / Vd) .* exp.(-ke .* t)

plot(t, C, lw = 2.4, color = :firebrick, label = "C(t)",
     xlabel = "Time (h)", ylabel = "Concentration (mg/L)",
     title = "Julia — one-compartment IV bolus", legend = :topright)
hline!([5], ls = :dash, color = :grey, label = "MEC")






Figure 1: Julia + Plots.jl: concentration–time curve.

title: Julia Engine
text:
An algorithm: Newton–Raphson root finding
Solve for the time at which concentration reaches the 5 mg/L threshold. (The cell is self-contained.)

dose, Vd, ke = 1000.0, 50.0, log(2) / 12   # constants

f(x)  = (dose / Vd) * exp(-ke * x) - 5.0
df(x) = -ke * (dose / Vd) * exp(-ke * x)

x = 10.0
for _ in 1:20
    x -= f(x) / df(x)
end
println("Threshold crossed at t = ", round(x, digits = 2), " h")
println("Analytic solution      = ", round(log((dose / Vd) / 5) / ke, digits = 2), " h")

Threshold crossed at t = 24.0 h
Analytic solution      = 24.0 h

title: Reveal.js slides — a deck that runs your code
text: This is where the story began — a badly aligned PowerPoint slide, decades ago. Today the same idea becomes a Reveal.js deck: authored in Markdown, with every figure generated by code at render, and presented full-screen in any browser.

title: Reveal.js slides — a deck that runs your code
text:
A slide is a heading
---
format: revealjs
---

## Why Quarto slides?
- Author in Markdown
- **Executable** code makes figures live
- Export to PDF with one flag

## A computed figure
```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.plot([0,1,2],[0,1,4]); plt.show()
```
Each ## starts a new slide. Incremental bullets, speaker notes, fragments, transitions and a logo are all one line of YAML each. Press E in the deck to export to PDF; the same source can also target PowerPoint (pptx) or Beamer for LaTeX purists.






TipPresent it now



Open the live Reveal.js deck — arrow keys to advance, F for full-screen, S for speaker notes.

title: Quarto dashboards — a clinical cockpit from code
text: Sometimes the output is not a paper but a dashboard — headline numbers, a chart and a table, laid out as a cockpit. Quarto builds these from the same Markdown + code you already know, with format: dashboard.

title: Quarto dashboards — a clinical cockpit from code
section: Layout by headings, content by code
text:
Layout by headings, content by code
---
title: "Anticoagulation Service Dashboard"
format: dashboard
---
## Row {height=20%}
```{python}
#| content: valuebox
#| title: "Patients in range"
dict(value="78%", icon="heart-pulse", color="success")
```

## Row
```{python}
# a matplotlib chart fills this card
```
```{python}
# a pandas table fills the next card
```
Rows and columns come from heading levels; #| content: valuebox turns a cell into a KPI tile. The result is responsive and self-contained — no dashboard framework, no server.






TipOpen the live dashboard



See the anticoagulation dashboard — value boxes, a computed chart and a data table, all from one .qmd.

title: The Python engine — Jupyter under the hood
text: When a document is rendered, Quarto hands every ```{python} block to a Jupyter kernel, executes it, and embeds the result — a figure, a table, a value — back into the page. Nothing is copied and pasted, so nothing can go stale.

title: The Python engine — Jupyter under the hood
text:
The idea in three lines
A Python code cell is written with a {python} engine tag and optional YAML-style options after #|:
```{python}
#| label: fig-pk
#| fig-cap: "Plasma concentration after a 1000 mg IV bolus."
import numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt
t = np.linspace(0, 48, 400)
C = (1000/50) * np.exp(-np.log(2)/12 * t)
plt.plot(t, C, color="#B71C1C", lw=2.2); plt.show()
```
#| label: fig-pk makes the figure cross-referenceable — write @fig-pk anywhere and Quarto inserts the correct number and a hyperlink.

title: The Python engine — Jupyter under the hood
text:
Why it matters clinically
Every pharmacokinetic curve, every summary table, every p-value is regenerated from the data each time the paper is built. Re-run with new numbers and the prose, figures and tables all move together. That is reproducibility you can defend in front of a reviewer.






TipSee it running live



The Python engine page executes this pharmacokinetic model in full — curve, numerical AUC, and a pandas table — all at render time.


The environment is pinned outside the synced Drive folder (~/.venvs/quarto) so package installs never trigger a cloud-sync storm.

title: The Julia engine — speed for heavy computation
text: Some problems — large matrix differential equations, Monte-Carlo simulations, population PK models — are simply slow in interpreted languages. Julia compiles to native code and runs close to C speed, and Quarto speaks it natively.

title: The Julia engine — speed for heavy computation
text:
Same document, a third language
```{julia}
using Plots
gr()
t = 0:0.5:48
C = 20.0 .* exp.(-(log(2)/12) .* t)
plot(t, C, lw = 2.4, color = :firebrick, xlabel = "Time (h)")
```
The remarkable part is not Julia alone — it is that an R chunk, a Python chunk and a Julia chunk can sit in one source file, each doing what it is best at, and render into one seamless document.






TipSee it running live



The Julia engine page executes a Plots.jl computation at render time. The project’s Julia environment is pinned via Project.toml / Manifest.toml so the result is fully reproducible.








Note



Julia’s first render compiles its packages and can take a minute; Quarto’s freeze then caches the result so subsequent builds are instant.

title: EPUB e-books — a chapter that opens in Apple Books
text: Seeing a chapter I had written, coded and published myself appear in Apple Books was a genuinely remarkable moment. Quarto builds the EPUB directly.

title: EPUB e-books — a chapter that opens in Apple Books
section: From Markdown to a real e-book
text:
From Markdown to a real e-book
---
title: "HbS trait vs β-thalassaemia trait"
author: "Dr Abdul Mannan"
format:
  epub:
    cover-image: cover.png
    css: epub-styles.css
    toc: true
---
quarto render explainer.qmd --to epub
The result is a reflowable EPUB — a proper cover, styled typography, a linked table of contents and chapters — that opens in Apple Books, Calibre or any reader, and resizes to any screen. A single explainer becomes a distributable e-book.






TipDownload the e-book



Grab the HbS vs β-thal EPUB and open it in Apple Books. Source and cover live in outputs/epub-hbs-hba2/.

title: Knowledge Has No Limits: From a Misaligned PowerPoint to a Complete Quarto Publishing System
text:
NoteAuthor’s Reflection




“Knowledge has no limits, and learning has no finishing line.”
— Dr Abdul Mannan

title: Knowledge Has No Limits: From a Misaligned PowerPoint to a Complete Quarto Publishing System
text:
1. R Engine ({r}): Kaplan-Meier Survival Curve
Using R and ggplot2, we generate a publication-ready Kaplan-Meier survival curve for clinical trials:


Code
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(2026)
time <- seq(0, 36, by=1)
surv_control <- exp(-0.06 * time) + rnorm(length(time), 0, 0.01)
surv_treated <- exp(-0.025 * time) + rnorm(length(time), 0, 0.01)

df <- data.frame(
  Time = rep(time, 2),
  Survival = c(pmax(0, surv_control), pmax(0, surv_treated)),
  Arm = factor(rep(c("Standard Care", "Targeted Therapy"), each=length(time)))
)

ggplot(df, aes(x=Time, y=Survival, color=Arm, linetype=Arm)) +
  geom_line(linewidth=1.2) +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("#7f0000", "#2e7d32")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent, limits=c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title="Overall Survival in Relapsed/Refractory Disease",
       subtitle="Kaplan-Meier Survival Estimates by Treatment Arm",
       x="Follow-up Duration (Months)", y="Overall Survival (%)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size=12) +
  theme(legend.position="bottom", plot.title=element_text(face="bold", color="#7f0000"))








Figure 1: Kaplan-Meier overall survival curve generated dynamically with R and ggplot2.





As demonstrated in Figure 1, targeted therapy significantly extends overall survival compared to standard care.

title: Knowledge Has No Limits: From a Misaligned PowerPoint to a Complete Quarto Publishing System
text:
2. R Engine ({r}): Meta-Analysis Forest Plot
Forest plots synthesize relative risk or hazard ratios across clinical studies:


Code
trials <- data.frame(
  Study = c("UK Haem Study (2022)", "EHA Registry (2024)", "ASH Consortium (2025)", "Blood Doctor Cohort (2026)", "Pooled Meta-Analysis"),
  HR = c(0.68, 0.72, 0.61, 0.58, 0.65),
  Lower = c(0.51, 0.58, 0.45, 0.42, 0.54),
  Upper = c(0.91, 0.89, 0.82, 0.80, 0.78),
  Summary = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)
)

ggplot(trials, aes(x=HR, y=reorder(Study, c(5,4,3,2,1)))) +
  geom_vline(xintercept=1, linetype="dashed", color="grey40") +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin=Lower, xmax=Upper), height=0.25, color="#7f0000", linewidth=1) +
  geom_point(aes(shape=Summary, size=Summary, fill=Summary), color="#7f0000") +
  scale_shape_manual(values=c(21, 23)) +
  scale_size_manual(values=c(3.5, 5)) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("#7f0000", "#ffd5d5")) +
  labs(title="Forest Plot of Treatment Hazard Ratios",
       x="Hazard Ratio (95% CI)", y=NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size=12) +
  theme(legend.position="none", axis.text.y=element_text(face="bold"))








Figure 2: Clinical Trial Forest Plot showing Hazard Ratios (HR) and 95% Confidence Intervals.

title: Knowledge Has No Limits: From a Misaligned PowerPoint to a Complete Quarto Publishing System
section: 3. Python engine ({python}) — pharmacokinetics & a pandas table
text:
3. Python engine ({python}) — pharmacokinetics & a pandas table
The same workflow speaks Python. This is the source that models one-compartment IV-bolus kinetics and builds a pandas summary table:
import numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt, pandas as pd

dose, Vd, t_half = 1000.0, 50.0, 12.0
ke = np.log(2) / t_half
t  = np.linspace(0, 48, 200)
C  = (dose / Vd) * np.exp(-ke * t)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.8))
ax.plot(t, C, color="#B71C1C", lw=2.2, label="Concentration C(t)")
ax.axhline(5, ls="--", color="grey", label="MEC (5 mg/L)")
ax.fill_between(t, C, 5, where=(C > 5), alpha=0.15, color="#B71C1C")
ax.set(xlabel="Time (h)", ylabel="Plasma concentration (mg/L)")
ax.legend(); ax.grid(alpha=0.3); plt.show()






TipWhy the Python figure isn’t drawn here



A Quarto document runs one execution engine at a time. This page is driven by R’s knitr — which is why the survival curve and forest plot above are live. The identical Python model runs live on its own page: watch it compute the curve, the numerical AUC and the table on the Python engine page, and drive the clinical dashboard.

title: Knowledge Has No Limits: From a Misaligned PowerPoint to a Complete Quarto Publishing System
section: 4. Julia engine ({julia}) — speed for heavy computation
text:
4. Julia engine ({julia}) — speed for heavy computation
For numerically expensive models, Julia compiles to near-C speed:
using Plots
gr()
t = 0:0.5:48
C = 20.0 .* exp.(-(log(2)/12) .* t)

plot(t, C, lw=2.4, color=:firebrick, xlabel="Time (h)",
     ylabel="Concentration (mg/L)", legend=:topright)






TipSee the Julia engine execute



The Julia engine page runs this Plots.jl code live at render, with the environment pinned by Project.toml / Manifest.toml.

title: Knowledge Has No Limits: From a Misaligned PowerPoint to a Complete Quarto Publishing System
text:
5. Observable JS Engine ({ojs}): Client-Side Interactivity
Observable JS (ojs) runs natively in the reader’s browser with zero backend server requirement:


Code
viewof half_life = Inputs.range([2, 48], {value: 12, step: 1, label: "Adjust Half-life t½ (hours):"})









Code
ke_ojs = Math.log(2) / half_life
data_ojs = d3.range(0, 48.5, 0.5).map(t => ({t, C: (1000 / 50) * Math.exp(-ke_ojs * t)}))

Plot.plot({
  width: 700, height: 320,
  x: {label: "Time (hours) →"},
  y: {label: "↑ Concentration (mg/L)", grid: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([5], {stroke: "grey", strokeDasharray: "4"}),
    Plot.areaY(data_ojs, {x: "t", y: "C", fill: "#B71C1C", fillOpacity: 0.15}),
    Plot.lineY(data_ojs, {x: "t", y: "C", stroke: "#B71C1C", strokeWidth: 2.2})
  ]
})

























Code
md`Dynamic Calculation: At **t½ = ${half_life} hours**, the elimination rate constant is **kₑ = ${ke_ojs.toFixed(4)} h⁻¹**.`

title: Knowledge Has No Limits: From a Misaligned PowerPoint to a Complete Quarto Publishing System
text:
6. Diagrams & Algorithms ({mermaid})
Quarto compiles plain-text Mermaid code blocks into static, vector SVG diagrams:






flowchart TD
    A["Patient Chromatogram Evaluation"] --> B{"HbS Present?"}
    B -->|Yes| C["Evaluate % HbS"]
    B -->|No| D["Evaluate % HbA2"]
    
    C -->|HbS 38–45%| E["Sickle Cell Trait (HbAS)"]
    C -->|HbS < 35%| F["HbAS + Concurrent α-Thal Trait"]
    D -->|HbA2 3.5–7.0%| G["β-Thalassaemia Trait"]

    style E fill:#edf8f0,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#d68b00,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#fff0f0,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px




Figure 3: Clinical decision algorithm compiled directly from plain text.

title: Knowledge Has No Limits: From a Misaligned PowerPoint to a Complete Quarto Publishing System
text:
7. Mathematical Equations & Citation Integrity
Mathematical expressions render using LaTeX / MathJax formatting:
\[\text{Hazard Ratio (HR)} = \frac{h_1(t)}{h_0(t)} = \exp\left( \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \dots \right)\]
\[\text{AUC}_{0 \to \infty} = \int_{0}^{\infty} C(t) \, dt = \frac{C_0}{k_e} = \frac{Dose}{CL}\]
Citations are linked to verified PubMed entries per the Citation Integrity Protocol (Donze et al. 1997; Mrabet et al. 1985).

title: Knowledge has no limits.
text:
A reproducible publishing lab




From a badly aligned PowerPoint slide on a hospital laboratory computer in the early 2000s — to coding my own books, reports, websites, dashboards and scientific figures. This blog is that journey, and a live demonstration of everything Quarto can build from a single source file.
Read the story ↓    Browse the gallery ↓

title: Knowledge has no limits.
text:
The gallery
Every card below is a working example. Each output type — from survival curves to EPUB e-books that open in Apple Books — is built, rendered and reproducible. Filter by category, or open a post to read the code that made it.

title: About this blog
text:
I am Dr Abdul Mannan, a Consultant Haematologist and Director of the Bangor Haemophilia Centre. This blog is two things at once: a personal record of learning to publish with code, and a living demonstration of what Quarto can build.
Every post here is a genuine, rendered example — not a screenshot. The survival curves are computed in R, the pharmacokinetic models in Python, the interactive sliders in your own browser, and the manuscripts, slides, book, EPUB and dashboard are all built from the same plain-text sources you can read in each post.

title: About this blog
text:
Why I built it
For most of my career, turning an idea into a finished scientific product meant depending on a publisher, a statistician or a designer — and on costly proprietary software. Quarto, with R and Python, removed many of those barriers. The analysis, the narrative, the references, the figures and the final publication now live in one reproducible workflow that I control.
Tools do not replace sound statistical judgement, scientific review or good design. But they remove the friction between an idea and its final form.







Do not be embarrassed by your first attempt. Every polished skill begins with an imperfect beginning. Keep learning. Keep building. Keep creating.










ImportantCitation integrity



Bibliographic references in these demonstration posts illustrate the mechanism. Before any real publication, every citation is re-verified live against PubMed and Crossref under my Citation Integrity Protocol.

title: Diagrams as code — Mermaid & Graphviz
text: Drawing a flowchart by hand in PowerPoint is slow, and the moment guidance changes you redraw it. Quarto lets you write diagrams as text and compiles them to scalable vector graphics.

title: Diagrams as code — Mermaid & Graphviz
section: A decision algorithm in plain text
text:
A decision algorithm in plain text
```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A["Chromatogram"] --> B{"HbS present?"}
  B -->|Yes| C["Evaluate % HbS"]
  B -->|No|  D["Evaluate % HbA2"]
  C -->|38–45%| E["Sickle cell trait"]
  D -->|3.5–7%| G["β-thalassaemia trait"]
```
That text renders as a labelled, colour-coded flowchart. Because it is text, it diffs cleanly in git — you can see exactly how a pathway changed between versions, and reviewers can comment on a line.
Mermaid covers flowcharts, sequence diagrams, Gantt charts and mind-maps; Graphviz (dot) handles dense graphs and inheritance trees. Both are built in.






TipSee the gallery



The Diagrams & algorithms page compiles several clinical pathways, including the HbS / HbA2 interpretation tree.

title: Interactive teaching documents — tabsets & callouts
text: A good teaching page reveals detail on demand. Quarto ships the building blocks as simple Markdown fences — no JavaScript to write.

title: Interactive teaching documents — tabsets & callouts
text:
Tabsets: one concept, several views
::: {.panel-tabset}
## Film        …blood-film description…
## Immunophenotype  …markers…
## Management   …first-line therapy…
:::
Readers click between Film, Immunophenotype and Management without leaving the page — ideal for comparing differentials.

title: Interactive teaching documents — tabsets & callouts
text:
Callouts carry the signal
::: {.callout-warning}
## Red flag
Blasts + coagulopathy → treat as APL until proven otherwise.
:::
Five callout styles (note, tip, important, warning, caution) give teaching material a consistent visual grammar, and collapsible callouts hide a model answer until the learner is ready.






TipSee it live



The interactive teaching demo combines tabsets, callouts and a live Observable widget in one lesson page.

title: Observable JS — interactivity with no server
text: R, Python and Julia run when the document is built. Observable JS (ojs) runs when the document is read — in the reader’s browser. That means genuine interactivity on a plain static website, with no server to host or maintain.

title: Observable JS — interactivity with no server
text:
A reactive cell
```{ojs}
viewof half_life = Inputs.range([2, 48], {value: 12, label: "t½ (hours)"})
```
```{ojs}
Plot.plot({ marks: [Plot.lineY(
  d3.range(0, 48, 0.5).map(t => ({t, C: 20*Math.exp(-Math.log(2)/half_life*t)})),
  {x: "t", y: "C", stroke: "#B71C1C"})] })
```
Drag the slider and every cell that depends on it recomputes instantly — the reactive model made famous by Observable, built into Quarto and powered by d3 and Observable Plot.

title: Observable JS — interactivity with no server
text:
Why teachers love it
A trainee can feel how halving a drug’s half-life changes its concentration curve, or how a diagnostic threshold shifts sensitivity and specificity — by moving a control, not by reading a caption.






TipTry it live



The Observable page and the flagship essay both carry a working half-life slider you can drag right now.

title: A Quarto book — chapters, cross-refs and an index
text: A book is more than a long document: it needs numbered chapters, a table of contents, cross-chapter references and a single bibliography. Quarto’s book project type handles all of it.

title: A Quarto book — chapters, cross-refs and an index
section: The whole book is a list of chapters
text:
The whole book is a list of chapters
project:
  type: book
book:
  title: "A Short Haematology Handbook"
  author: "Dr Abdul Mannan"
  chapters:
    - index.qmd
    - intro.qmd
    - anaemia.qmd
    - references.qmd
format:
  html: default
  pdf:  { documentclass: scrreprt }
quarto render book            # → an HTML book website
quarto render book --to pdf   # → a typeset PDF
Reference a figure in Chapter 3 from Chapter 1 and the number resolves itself. The output is simultaneously a navigable website and a print-ready PDF — the same content, two finished products.






TipRead the demo book



Open the rendered handbook — a complete multi-chapter Quarto book, built from four .qmd files.

title: The R engine — knitr, ggplot2 and the tidyverse
text: Quarto’s R support is the mature knitr engine that powered R Markdown for a decade. Anything you can do in R — ggplot2, survival, gtsummary, meta — you can do inside a document, with the output embedded automatically.

title: The R engine — knitr, ggplot2 and the tidyverse
text:
A Kaplan–Meier curve, from code
```{r}
#| label: fig-km
#| fig-cap: "Overall survival by treatment arm."
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(df, aes(Time, Survival, colour = Arm)) +
  geom_step(linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("#7f0000", "#2e7d32")) +
  theme_minimal()
```
Change the dataset, re-render, and the curve redraws itself. The same pattern produces meta-analysis forest plots, gtsummary Table 1s, and regression outputs — the staples of a haematology paper.

title: The R engine — knitr, ggplot2 and the tidyverse
text:
Freedom from licences
R and its 20,000 packages are open source. For most analyses I run in clinic and research, I am no longer tied to a costly proprietary statistics licence: the computation, the figure and the manuscript now live in one free toolchain.






TipSee it running live



The R engine page renders a survival curve and a forest plot from scratch. The flagship essay also embeds both, side by side with Python, Julia and Observable.

title: One source, three manuscripts — HTML, PDF & Word
text: The oldest friction in academic writing is format juggling: the journal wants a Word file, your co-author wants a PDF, the pre-print server wants HTML. Quarto produces all three from one source.

title: One source, three manuscripts — HTML, PDF & Word
text:
Declare the formats, render once
---
title: "A reproducible haematology manuscript"
format:
  html:  default
  pdf:   { documentclass: scrartcl }
  docx:  default
---
Then:
quarto render manuscript.qmd            # builds every format
quarto render manuscript.qmd --to docx  # or just one
Cross-references (@fig-, @tbl-), citations from a .bib file, and figure numbering all travel across formats automatically. Send the Word file to a co-author for track-changes; submit the PDF to the journal; post the HTML as a pre-print — never re-formatting by hand.






TipOpen the rendered outputs



HTML · PDF · Word (.docx) — all three built from the one file.

title: Observable JS Engine
text:
Observable JS (OJS) cells run entirely in the reader’s browser and react to each other automatically. d3 and Plot are built in — nothing to install.
Drag the slider: the pharmacokinetic curve updates live.

viewof thalf = Inputs.range([2, 48], {value: 12, step: 1, label: "Half-life t½ (h)"})







ke = Math.log(2) / thalf
data = d3.range(0, 48.5, 0.5).map(t => ({t, C: (1000 / 50) * Math.exp(-ke * t)}))
















Plot.plot({
  width: 700, height: 340,
  x: {label: "Time (h) →"},
  y: {label: "↑ Concentration (mg/L)", grid: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleY([5], {stroke: "grey", strokeDasharray: "4"}),
    Plot.areaY(data, {x: "t", y: "C", fill: "#B71C1C", fillOpacity: 0.12}),
    Plot.lineY(data, {x: "t", y: "C", stroke: "#B71C1C", strokeWidth: 2.2})
  ]
})







md`At **t½ = ${thalf} h**, the elimination rate constant is
**kₑ = ${ke.toFixed(4)} h⁻¹** and the concentration falls below the
5 mg/L threshold at about **${(Math.log((1000/50)/5)/ke).toFixed(1)} h**.`









 Back to top

title: R Engine — knitr
text: The knitr engine executes R chunks. Packages are loaded from your personal library (~/Library/R/arm64/4.5/library). Here we use ggplot2.

title: R Engine — knitr
text:
Simulated platelet recovery after chemotherapy

library(ggplot2)
set.seed(42)

days      <- 0:28
platelets <- 20 + 380 / (1 + exp(-0.30 * (days - 14)))   # logistic recovery
obs       <- platelets + rnorm(length(days), 0, 9)
df        <- data.frame(day = days, platelets = obs)

ggplot(df, aes(day, platelets)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 100, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_line(colour = "#B71C1C", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(colour = "#7f0000", size = 1.8) +
  labs(title = "Simulated platelet recovery",
       x = "Day post-chemotherapy",
       y = expression(Platelets~(10^9/L))) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)







Figure 1: Modelled platelet recovery; dashed line marks the 100 ×10⁹/L threshold.

title: R Engine — knitr
text:
A quick summary table

knitr::kable(
  data.frame(
    Milestone = c("Nadir", "Threshold crossed", "Day 28"),
    Day       = c(which.min(platelets) - 1,
                  days[which(platelets >= 100)[1]],
                  28),
    Platelets = round(c(min(platelets),
                        platelets[which(platelets >= 100)[1]],
                        tail(platelets, 1)))
  ),
  caption = "Key points of the recovery curve."
)


Key points of the recovery curve.

title: Interactive Teaching Document
text: This mirrors your single-file HTML learning apps, but authored in Markdown and fully reproducible.

title: Interactive Teaching Document
text:
Layered content with tabsets

ConceptMechanismMonitoring


Emicizumab is a bispecific antibody bridging activated factor IX and factor X, mimicking the cofactor function of factor VIII.


It does not require activation and is not affected by FVIII inhibitors — hence its role in haemophilia A with inhibitors.


Standard APTT-based assays are unreliable on emicizumab. Use a bovine-based chromogenic FVIII assay to monitor.

title: Interactive Teaching Document
text:
Callouts for emphasis






WarningAssay pitfall



Emicizumab shortens the APTT dramatically and invalidates one-stage FVIII assays.








NoteExpand: dosing summary





Loading 3 mg/kg weekly ×4, then a maintenance schedule (weekly / fortnightly / four-weekly). Verify against the current SmPC before clinical use.

title: Interactive Teaching Document
text:
A live interactive: dose–exposure explorer

viewof weight = Inputs.range([10, 120], {value: 70, step: 1, label: "Body weight (kg)"})







loading = 3.0 * weight
md`A **3 mg/kg** loading dose for a **${weight} kg** patient is
**${loading.toFixed(0)} mg** per weekly dose during the 4-week loading phase.`
